rulings.Case Title: IN RE: ESTATE OF FRANCISCO CRISOSTOMO
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR The Posting Of The Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 2] Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on January 20, 2021. Petitioner is ordered to lodge with the clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with this tentative by the time set for this hearing. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. The appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 2 of 2]

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: ESTATE OF FRANCISCO CRISOSTOMO
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Transfer Venue To Alameda County
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] The tentative ruling is entered by Judge Ross C. Moody. Two issues are before the Court, Respondent's motion to transfer venue to Alameda County, and his request for sanctions. This tentative ruling will address each in turn. The motion to transfer venue appears to have been rendered moot by Petitioner's request to dismiss the pending petition in San Francisco filed January 19, 2021. The Court is in receipt of the request to dismiss, as well as the January 19, 2021 letter from Respondent's counsel to the Director of Probate asking that the motion not be removed from the calendar as moot, and specifically seeking a ruling on the merits of his sanctions request. Unless the Probate Code provides otherwise, the rules of practice applicable to civil actions apply to probate proceedings. (Prob. C. section 1000.) Generally, a petitioner has the right to enter a voluntary dismissal under Code of Civil Procedure section 581. (See Estate of Cook (1928) 205 Cal. 581, 587.) The Court intends to dismiss the petition as requested, but only after adjudicating the sanctions request on the merits. Respondent's sanctions request is DENIED. Under CCP section 396b(b), the Court may order payment of reasonable expenses and attorney's fees to the party making or opposing a motion to transfer and from the opposing attorney personally, depending upon: "(1) whether an offer to stipulate to change of venue was reasonably made and rejected, and (2) whether the motion or selection of venue was made in good faith given the facts and law the party making the motion or selecting the venue knew or should have known. As between the party and his or her attorney, those expenses and fees shall be the personal liability of the attorney not chargeable to the party." (CCP section 396b(b).) Here, it was not clearly erroneous to bring a petition in San Francisco regarding the powers of attorney. As Respondent acknowledges, the action was filed in the county where he as the named agent resides, in keeping with Probate Code sections 4523 and 4763. Even in the letter from Respondent's counsel to Petitioner's counsel, there is an acknowledgment that the petitions filed in this county were not strictly improper: "From what we understand, your client's petitions in San Francisco seek relief that is more properly sought by [sic] an Alameda County Court." (November 5, 2020 letter, second paragraph, first sentence.) In conclusion, the Court finds that the offer to stipulate to change in venue was not unreasonably rejected, and that the selection of San Francisco for the Petition on matters relating to acts and transactions of the agent or attorney-in-fact was not made in bad faith based on the face of the relevant statutes (Probate sections 4523, 4763). Because the offer to stipulate to the change of venue was not unreasonably rejected, and there is no showing that the petition was not brought in good faith based on the facts and the law, the Court declines to order the requested sanctions. (CCP section 396b(b)). [Part 1 of 2]
